name: Haiti (Republic of Haiti / République d'Haïti / Repiblik Ayiti)
summary: Haiti is a Caribbean civil-law republic whose family-law framework operates under the Civil Code (Code Civil, drawing on French Napoleonic Code substantive heritage via 1825 adoption) supplemented by the Decree of 8 October 1982 on Marital Equality and the 2014 Children's Rights Code (Code de Protection de l'Enfant). Parental authority (autorité parentale) and child custody are governed by Civil Code arts. 367-389. The Court of Cassation (Cour de Cassation) is the apex court for civil and criminal matters; the Superior Court of Auditors and Administrative Disputes (Cour Supérieure des Comptes et du Contentieux Administratif) operates within the administrative jurisdiction. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in the Court of First Instance (Tribunal de Première Instance). Psychology profession is regulated through the Association Haïtienne de Psychologie under Ministry of Public Health framework. Haiti is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the interest-of-the-child standard. Haiti acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 February 2013.

statutory_framework.title: Civil Code — Parental authority and custody
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Civil Code drawing on French Napoleonic Code substantive heritage via 1825 adoption. Arts. 367-389 govern autorité parentale and child custody.

statutory_framework.citation: Decree of 8 October 1982 on Marital Equality
statutory_framework.title: Decree on Marital Equality
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Decree codifying marital equality and parental responsibility framework.

statutory_framework.citation: Children's Rights Code 2014 (Code de Protection de l'Enfant)
statutory_framework.title: Children's Rights Code
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Code aligned with UNCRC obligations.

apex_courts.name: Court of Cassation (Cour de Cassation)
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters.

professional_regulators.name: Association Haïtienne de Psychologie (AHPSY)
professional_regulators.role: Peak professional association for psychologists in Haiti.

anonymisation_convention: Haitian family-court decisions are anonymised per Court of Cassation practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Haitian independence
key_developments.description: Haiti declared independence — first Black republic and second oldest independent republic in Americas after United States.

key_developments.title: Civil Code adoption (French Napoleonic)
key_developments.description: Federal Civil Code adopted via French Napoleonic Code substantive heritage.

key_developments.title: Decree on Marital Equality
key_developments.description: Federal Decree codifying marital equality and parental responsibility framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession
key_developments.description: Haiti acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 February 2013.

key_developments.title: Children's Rights Code
key_developments.description: Federal Children's Rights Code enacted aligned with UNCRC obligations.

structural_findings: Haiti operates a French-Napoleonic-derivative civil-law family-law framework — places Haiti alongside Dominican Republic within the Hispaniola civil-law cluster within the Caribbean.
Haitian independence (1804) is the earliest in the Americas after the United States — structurally distinctive within the corpus.
Bilingual official-language framework (French + Haitian Creole) is structurally distinctive within the Caribbean cluster.
Hague Convention 1980 accession 2013 places Haiti in the Hague Caribbean cluster.
editorial_notes: Haiti jurisdiction sidecar — civil-law Caribbean (Civil Code Napoleonic-derivative + Decree on Marital Equality 1982 + Children's Rights Code 2014 + bilingual French-Haitian Creole + Hague Convention 1980 accession 2013).
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator.
Joins Caribbean + civil-law + Napoleonic-derivative + earliest-Americas-Black-republic-distinctive + bilingual-Creole + Hague Convention clusters within the corpus.
